1: A revelation of God: the crossing of the Sea of Galilee positions Christ's feeding miracle in a geography already loaded with Exodus memory — the sea-crossing that defines Israel's redemption. The Evangelist does not belabor the echo, but the spatial setting primes the reader for the wilderness-and-bread typology that will dominate the chapter.
2: The theme of sign and crowd: the gathering of "a great crowd" who followed because of Christ's healing signs anticipates the prophetic pattern of the nations streaming to the one whom God has vindicated (Isaiah 2:2–3; John 60:3). But the Evangelist will shortly reveal that following signs for their benefits falls short of the faith the signs are designed to generate.
3: A shadow: Christ ascending the mountain and sitting down to teach his disciples evokes the pattern of Moses receiving and delivering Torah from Sinai (Exodus 19:20; Deuteronomy 5:5). The one who is about to provide bread in the wilderness does so from a mountain — positioning him as greater than Moses before the explicit contrast is drawn in the discourse.
4: A shadow: the Passover approaching as the frame for the feeding miracle is not incidental detail. The Passover — the meal of the lamb whose blood averted judgment, whose eating constituted the redeemed community — is the typological key to the Bread of Life discourse that follows. The Evangelist announces the feast early so the reader receives the miracle within its proper interpretive register.
5: A revelation of God: Christ already knowing what he will do (John 6:6) while asking Philip "where shall we buy bread?" enacts the divine pattern of testing — God asks questions he already knows the answer to in order to draw out the limits of human resource and the necessity of divine provision (cf. Genesis 3:9; Exodus 16:4). The question is not a request for information but an invitation to discover inadequacy.
6: A revelation of God: the aside that Jesus "already had in mind what he was going to do" establishes divine foreordination as the baseline of the miracle. The Evangelist consistently shows Christ operating from sovereign intention, not reactive improvisation. The feeding of the multitude is not a fortunate solution to a crisis but a planned revelation of who Christ is.
7: A revelation of God: Philip's arithmetic ("more than half a year's wages") measures the problem in human categories and finds it insoluble. The inadequacy is the point — divine provision operates precisely at the boundary where human resource fails. The pattern runs from the widow's jar of oil (2 Kings 4:1–7) to the manna whose supply exceeded human calculation. Christ is about to exceed human arithmetic again.
8: A revelation of God: Andrew's role as the one who finds and brings the small resource (the boy with five loaves and two fish) mirrors the pattern of God using humble instruments to display divine power. His confession of inadequacy — "but how far will they go among so many?" — is itself a form of honesty that opens space for the miracle. The Evangelist notes the meagerness to amplify the sufficiency of what follows.
9: A type: five barley loaves and two fish in the hands of one person among five thousand directly recalls Elisha's feeding miracle at Gilgal (2 Kings 4:42–44) — a man with twenty barley loaves fed one hundred people, with food left over. The structural parallel is deliberate, but Christ's miracle exceeds Elisha's by an order of magnitude, establishing that the one greater than the prophet has arrived.
10: A revelation of God: the command to have the people sit down on the grass — with the grass specifically noted — echoes Psalms 23:2 ("he makes me lie down in green pastures") and the shepherd imagery that Christ will explicitly claim later in the Gospel (John 10:11). The host who seats the guests is the Lord who provides; the Evangelist places Christ in the shepherd's role before the Bread of Life discourse names it.
11: A type: the action sequence — Jesus taking the loaves, giving thanks, distributing — employs the same fourfold pattern (take, bless, break, give) that the Synoptic tradition preserves in the institution of the Lord's Supper (Matthew 26:26; 1 Corinthians 11:23–24). The Evangelist, writing for a community that celebrates the eucharist, positions the feeding miracle as anticipating the meal Christ will give from his own body. The giving of thanks (eucharistein) is the verb the Supper would make permanently significant.
12: A revelation of God: the command to gather the fragments "so that nothing is lost" prefigures the language Christ will use of his elect: "I shall lose none of all those he has given me" (John 6:39). Divine provision does not waste; what God gives, God preserves to the end. The fragments gathered are a enacted parable of the preservation of the redeemed.
13: The theme of superabundance: twelve baskets of leftovers after five thousand have eaten is the signature of divine excess — God's provision does not merely meet the need but overwhelms it. The number twelve resonates with the twelve tribes of Israel; the Evangelist may intend the echo of a restored and abundantly provided-for Israel gathered around its Lord, echoing Ezekiel's vision of the land's restoration (Ezekiel 34:26–29).
14: A fulfillment: the crowd's identification of Jesus as "the Prophet who is to come into the world" is a direct application of Deuteronomy 18:15 — Moses's promise of a prophet like himself who would speak God's words and whom Israel must obey. The sign they witnessed (bread in the wilderness) triggers the comparison naturally: Moses brought manna; this one has brought bread. But the crowd has identified correctly without yet understanding fully what they have found.
15: A revelation of God: Christ withdrawing to the mountain alone when the crowd seeks to make him king by force establishes a pattern essential to understanding his Messianic identity. His kingdom is "not of this world" (John 18:36); political kingship forced by popular demand would be a distortion of the Messianic office, not its fulfillment. The withdrawal is not cowardice but sovereign self-definition — the king will receive his throne through the cross, not through acclamation.
16: A theme: the disciples going down to the lake as evening falls introduces the transition to the walking-on-water account. The darkness descending — the Evangelist notes "it was dark" in John 6:17 — is the Johannine signal of vulnerability and danger. Throughout the Gospel, darkness is the domain from which Christ's presence rescues; the night crossing sets the scene for a revelation of divine identity.
17: A revelation of God: the disciples are in the boat after dark, the sea is growing rough, and "Jesus had not yet joined them." The deliberate withholding — Christ remaining behind, the disciples rowing alone into the dark — creates the existential condition of absence that the epiphany will resolve. The Evangelist's pacing mirrors the shape of Psalms 107:23–30 (the seafarers in distress whom the Lord delivers).
18: A shadow: the strong wind and rough waters recall the deep-water chaos traditions of the OT — the seas as the domain of disorder that God alone masters (Psalms 89:9; Psalms 107:29). The storm is not merely meteorological; it is a setting that throughout the biblical narrative belongs to divine intervention. The chaos of wind and water is the precondition for the display of the one who commands them.
19: A direct revelation: Christ walking on the sea enacts what the OT reserves for God alone — Job 9:8 declares that God "alone treads on the waves of the sea," and Psalms 77:19 speaks of God's way being "through the sea, your path through the mighty waters." The Evangelist presents this not as a marvel of power but as an unveiling of identity: the one walking toward the disciples is doing what only the God of Israel does.
20: A direct claim: "It is I; don't be afraid" is the ego eimi declaration in its most compressed form — the same construction the Evangelist will use at John 8:58 ("before Abraham was, I am") and that the disciples will fall back from in Gethsemane (John 18:6). The divine name of Exodus 3:14 and its reinforcement in Isaiah 41:10 ("do not fear, for I am with you") converge: the one walking on the water is the covenant God who addresses his trembling people.
21: A revelation of God: the immediate arrival at the shore — the boat reaching its destination at the moment Christ enters it — is presented without elaboration. The Evangelist does not explain the mechanism; the point is that Christ's presence resolves what human effort could not. The disciples were straining at the oars in darkness; Christ's arrival is both rescue and destination. The pattern prefigures what his presence will mean for the community: arrival, not just passage.
22: A revelation of God: the crowd's careful reconstruction of the previous day's events — noting the single boat, the disciples' departure without Jesus — sets up their pursuit. The Evangelist records their confusion with an interest in the logic of discipleship: they are looking for Jesus, but for the wrong reasons (John 6:26). The pursuit that follows is the Evangelist's occasion for Christ to redirect from bread that perishes to bread that endures.
23: A theme: boats from Tiberias arriving near the site of the miracle — where "the Lord had given thanks" — is the Evangelist's subtle notation that the eucharistic dimensions of the feeding are part of its identity. He does not call Jesus "the Lord" often in the narrative; using the title here at the site of the thanksgiving-meal echoes the liturgical context in which the community knows this story.
24: A revelation of God: the crowd going to Capernaum to find Jesus does not know they are enacting the pattern of seeking God. The Evangelist positions the seeking-crowd as a type of the religious quest that Christ will redirect: people search for temporal sustenance and find the source of eternal life — if they will receive what is offered instead of what they came for.
25: A revelation of God: "Rabbi, when did you get here?" is the question of people who are following signs without grasping their significance. The crowd tracks Christ's movements but not his identity. The Evangelist uses the question to expose the gap between curiosity about Jesus and knowledge of who he is — a gap the Bread of Life discourse will attempt to close.
26: A revelation of God: Christ's diagnosis — "you are looking for me not because you saw the signs, but because you ate the loaves" — distinguishes between receiving a miracle as a provision and receiving it as a revelation. Signs in the Fourth Gospel point beyond themselves to the identity of the one who performs them. The crowd has grasped the bread but missed the Bread-giver, eaten the type but not recognized the antitype.
27: A shadow: "food that spoils" is the category that the manna belonged to — manna that was hoarded rotted (Exodus 16:20), and manna that was not gathered in time disappeared. The contrast Christ draws inverts the wilderness pattern: Israel was commanded to gather what would perish; Christ now commands pursuit of what endures to eternal life. The Son of Man — the Daniel 7 figure who receives eternal dominion — is the one who gives it.
28: A revelation of God: the crowd's question — "what must we do to do the works God requires?" — comes from the covenantal frame of Torah-observance: righteousness is a matter of doing the right deeds. Christ's answer will reframe the question entirely, naming the singular "work of God" as faith in the one God has sent. The Law-works framework gives way to the gift-and-reception framework.
29: A direct claim: "the work of God is this: to believe in the one he has sent." The singular work that fulfills the divine requirement is not a program of deeds but the reception of Christ. This inverts every law-centered scheme of righteousness. The Evangelist positions Christ here as the terminal point of all Israel's religious striving — not its abolition but its redefinition as faith in the Sent One.
30: A revelation of God: the crowd immediately asks for a sign to legitimate Christ's claim — unaware that the feeding of the five thousand was precisely that sign. The pattern of demand following miracle is the wilderness pattern: Israel saw God's acts and then tested him for more (Psalms 95:9; Exodus 17:7). The Evangelist shows that the generation of Christ repeats the pattern of its ancestors.
31: A direct echo: the crowd cites Psalms 78:24 (and Exodus 16:4) — "He gave them bread from heaven to eat" — presenting manna as the template that validates genuine divine provision. The citation is accurate but the inference is wrong: they expect Christ to replicate Moses's manna. The Evangelist uses the crowd's citation to set up Christ's decisive clarification that Moses did not give the true bread — God did, and is doing so now.
32: A direct Christological argument: "it was not Moses who gave you the bread from heaven, but it is my Father who gives you the true bread from heaven." Christ corrects the crowd's typology at two points: the agent was God, not Moses; and the true referent of the bread-from-heaven promise is not manna but what the Father is now providing. Moses was the servant; the one speaking is the Son who embodies what the servant's sign pointed toward.
33: A shadow: the manna gave life to one nation in the wilderness for forty years; the bread of God "gives life to the world." The universalizing move — from Israel to the world — echoes the servant songs of Isaiah (John 42:6; John 49:6) where the servant's mission extends beyond Israel to be "a light for the Gentiles." Christ is the one in whom the national gift becomes the world gift.
34: A revelation of God: "Sir, always give us this bread" mirrors the Samaritan woman's request for living water (John 4:15) — both requests are sincere but uncomprehending, asking for abundance within the category they already know (bread, water) rather than for the category-transcending gift Christ is offering. The requests do not offend Christ; they prompt the great ego eimi declarations.
35: A direct revelation: "I am the bread of life" — the first of the chapter's ego eimi declarations — is the Christological climax of the first movement of the discourse. The one of whom manna was a type, the one to whom the bread-from-heaven promise pointed, now names himself as that bread. "Whoever comes to me will never go hungry" fulfills the hunger-satisfying language of Isaiah 55:1–3 (come, buy, eat without price) in the person of Christ.
36: A revelation of God: "you have seen me and still you do not believe" diagnoses the problem as not intellectual but moral and spiritual — the failure to receive the bread is not a failure of evidence but a failure of will enabled by divine sovereignty (as John 6:37–40 will explain). The Evangelist positions unbelief as the default human condition that divine election overrides, not as a rational conclusion from insufficient data.
37: A revelation of God: "all those the Father gives me will come to me" establishes the Father's prior sovereign act as the ground of every movement toward Christ. The preservation clause — "whoever comes to me I will never drive away" — echoes the shepherd's commitment to his flock (Ps 23; Ezekiel 34:11–16) and anticipates the explicit Good Shepherd discourse (John 10). Not one given by the Father to the Son will be turned away.
38: A direct claim: "I have come down from heaven not to do my will but to do the will of him who sent me." The descent from heaven is the Incarnation stated as Christ's own testimony. The obedience to the Father's will within the Godhead — the Son doing not his own will but the Father's — provides the internal logic for the atonement: the cross is the supreme act of filial obedience, not compelled suffering from outside.
39: A revelation of God: "I shall lose none of all those he has given me, but raise them up at the last day." The language of not losing what the Father has given recalls the fragment-gathering of John 6:12 — nothing of what God provides is wasted. The resurrection promise ("raise them up at the last day") is the end-point of a preservation that begins with the Father's gift and runs through the Son's keeping to the final day.
40: A direct revelation: "everyone who looks to the Son and believes in him shall have eternal life, and I will raise them up at the last day." The looking-and-believing pattern recalls the bronze serpent (Numbers 21:9; John 3:14–15): those who looked at the lifted serpent lived. Here the looking is to the Son; the life given is not temporal preservation but eternal life. The type finds its antitype named explicitly.
41: A shadow: the Jewish leaders "grumbled" at Christ's claim to have come down from heaven — the same verb the LXX uses for Israel's wilderness grumbling (egoggyzon, Exodus 16:2; Numbers 14:2). The Evangelist signals the parallel deliberately: as Israel grumbled at God's provision in the wilderness, their descendants grumble at the one that provision pointed toward. The pattern of unbelief repeats across the generations.
42: A revelation of God: "is this not Jesus, the son of Joseph, whose father and mother we know?" — the offense of the Incarnation named in its sharpest form. The one who claims divine origin has a known human family. The Evangelist does not dismiss the objection but allows it to stand as the scandal it is: the Word became flesh, and the flesh is mundane enough to be identified by patronym and parentage. Familiarity becomes the obstacle to recognition.
43: A shadow: Christ's command to stop grumbling directly echoes Moses's reproach of Israel in the wilderness (Exodus 16:8 — "you are not grumbling against us, but against the LORD"). The Evangelist positions grumbling against Christ as grumbling against God — the same category the wilderness generation occupied. The one they grumble against is the Lord of the provision they are rejecting.
44: A revelation of God: "no one can come to me unless the Father who sent me draws them." The verb helkein (draw) is used of the powerful drawing of a net (John 21:6, 11) or a sword (John 18:10) — not gentle attraction but effective divine action. The sovereignty of grace is the ground of coming-to-Christ: the Father's drawing precedes and enables faith. The promise of resurrection at the last day is the guarantee that what the Father draws, the Son keeps.
45: A direct fulfillment: "It is written in the Prophets: 'They will all be taught by God.'" — a citation of Isaiah 54:13 (with overtones of Jeremiah 31:34, the new covenant promise that all will know the LORD). The prophecy of divine teaching is being fulfilled in the very discourse Christ is delivering: those who hear the Father and learn from him are being drawn to the Son. The Spirit who teaches is the Father's own instruction arriving through Christ.
46: A direct Christological claim: "no one has seen the Father except the one who is from God; only he has seen the Father." The claim establishes Christ's unique mediatorial position: the prophecy of Isaiah 54:13 (all taught by God) does not imply unmediated access to the Father — it is access mediated through the Son who alone has direct sight of the Father. Christ is both the content of the Father's teaching and the only path through which the Father is known.
47: A direct revelation: "Truly, truly, I tell you, the one who believes has eternal life." The amen amen formula marks the statement as solemn and authoritative — Christ speaking with the self-referential authority of the one who gives eternal life rather than merely announcing it. Belief in the Son of God is the condition on which the life of the age to come becomes present possession.
48: A direct revelation: "I am the bread of life" — the ego eimi returned in concentrated form after the exposition of John 6:37–47. The declaration is not a repetition but a resumption: the entire argument about the Father's will, the Son's preservation, the drawing of the elect, and the teaching of the Spirit has been the unpacking of what it means that Christ is the bread of life. The declaration now has depth it lacked at John 6:35.
49: A direct contrast: "your ancestors ate the manna in the wilderness, yet they died." Manna was real bread from God, but it was temporary provision for mortal bodies. It could not give what the body ultimately needs: life beyond physical death. The type served its purpose but was not the thing itself. Christ now names the limit of the type in order to establish what the antitype provides.
50: A shadow: "but here is the bread that comes down from heaven, which anyone may eat and not die." Manna came from heaven but did not prevent death; this bread comes from heaven and grants immunity to death. The universalizing "anyone" opens the gift beyond Israel — the bread is not limited to the wilderness generation or to ethnic Israel but is available to all who eat, echoing the Isaianic invitation (Isaiah 55:1, "come, all you who are thirsty").
51: A direct revelation: "I am the living bread that came down from heaven. Whoever eats this bread will live forever. This bread is my flesh, which I give for the life of the world." The movement from bread to flesh — from the material to the incarnate — is the climax of the first movement of the discourse. "My flesh, which I give for the life of the world" carries both Passover freight (the lamb whose flesh was eaten, whose blood averted judgment) and sin-offering resonance (the body given in substitution). The eucharistic anticipation is now explicit: the body Christ will give on the cross is the bread the community eats.
52: A revelation of God: the sharp dispute — "how can this man give us his flesh to eat?" — registers the literal incomprehension that the discourse requires. The Evangelist does not soften it. Christ will not retreat from the offense of the claim but intensify it (John 6:53–58), because the point is not that the language is figurative and easily digested but that the reality it names — union with Christ through his broken body and shed blood — is genuinely difficult and genuinely necessary.
53: A direct revelation: "unless you eat the flesh of the Son of Man and drink his blood, you have no life in you." The Son of Man title (Daniel 7:13–14) is now united with the Passover and covenant-meal imagery: the one who receives eternal dominion is the one whose flesh and blood become the food and drink of those who receive life. Drinking blood was scandalous to Jewish hearers (Leviticus 17:14); the shock is deliberate, forcing a re-categorization of what nourishment and life now mean.
54: A revelation of God: "whoever eats my flesh and drinks my blood has eternal life, and I will raise them up at the last day." The fourfold repetition of resurrection at the last day (John 6:39, 40, 44, 54) frames the entire discourse in eschatological promise. Participation in Christ — eating and drinking him — is not merely mystical union in the present but the ground of bodily resurrection at the eschaton. The meal is eschatological, not merely spiritual.
55: A direct claim: "for my flesh is real food and my blood is real drink." The adjective alēthēs (real, true, genuine) is the Johannine marker for the thing itself as opposed to its shadow or figure — the true vine (John 15:1), the true light (John 1:9), the true bread (John 6:32). Christ's flesh and blood are not figures of something else; they are the genuine nourishment that manna and sacrificial meals were figures of.
56: A revelation of God: "whoever eats my flesh and drinks my blood remains in me, and I in them." The language of mutual indwelling — the menein en formula — is the Johannine description of covenant union. The meal is not transactional but relational: eating Christ is the act through which the believer enters the dwelling-in-Christ that constitutes new covenant membership. The vine-branches image of chapter 15 is here anticipated in the meal framework.
57: A revelation of God: "just as the living Father sent me and I live because of the Father, so the one who feeds on me will live because of me." The chain of life — Father → Son → believer — runs through a sharing in the divine life at each link. The believer's participation in Christ is structurally analogous to the Son's participation in the Father: not identical (the Son is homoousios; the believer is creature), but genuinely sharing in the life that flows from its source.
58: A direct contrast: "this is the bread that came down from heaven. Your ancestors ate manna and died, but whoever feeds on this bread will live forever." The typological argument is brought to its conclusion: manna was real, was from God, was sufficient — but it was a sign, and signs are by nature surpassed by their referents. Feeding on Christ is the reality that the manna-sign pointed toward; where manna gave temporary life to mortal bodies, Christ gives life that is not terminated by death.
59: A revelation of God: the parenthetical notation that this teaching was given "in the synagogue in Capernaum" places Christ's most radical Christological claim in the heart of Jewish communal worship. He does not reserve the Bread of Life discourse for private instruction or sympathetic audiences; the claim to be the bread from heaven is made in the house of Israel's Scripture-centered gathering, to those most equipped to evaluate it by the categories he invokes.
60: A revelation of God: "this is a hard teaching. Who can accept it?" — the disciples' response is honest. The offense is not misunderstanding but understanding too well: Christ has claimed to be the divine bread whose eating is necessary for life, and has spoken of eating his flesh and drinking his blood. The hardness is the hardness of the gospel itself — not obscurity but the radical demand that the one who gives life must be received and consumed, not merely admired.
61: A revelation of God: Christ's awareness that his disciples were grumbling — "aware that his disciples were grumbling about this" — echoes the divine knowledge of Israel's murmuring in the wilderness (Exodus 16:7–9: "the LORD has heard your grumbling"). The Evangelist positions Christ in the role of the omniscient God who knows what his people say against him. His response does not soften the claim but sharpens it.
62: A direct claim: "what will you think if you see the Son of Man ascend to where he was before?" — the Ascension is invoked as the proof that would confirm what the Incarnation claim requires: the one who came down from heaven will return to where he came from. The Son of Man of Daniel 7:13–14 — who comes before the Ancient of Days — is here identified as the one who pre-existed in heaven before his descent. The Christological content of the Bread of Life discourse is grounded in a doctrine of pre-existence and return that the Ascension will vindicate.
63: A revelation of God: "the Spirit gives life; the flesh counts for nothing. The words I have spoken to you — they are full of the Spirit and life." Christ does not retract the flesh-and-blood language of John 6:51–58 but reframes how it operates: the eating is not cannibalistic but spiritual, and the instrument of life is the Spirit working through Christ's words. The same Spirit-flesh distinction that runs from Genesis 2 through Ezekiel 36:26–27 to Paul (Romans 8:6) governs the reception of Christ's discourse: it is received spiritually, not carnally.
64: A revelation of God: "for Jesus had known from the beginning which of them did not believe and who would betray him." The Evangelist's parenthetical notes about Christ's foreknowledge (John 2:24–25; John 6:15; John 13:11) establish that the drama of rejection and betrayal unfolds within sovereign divine knowledge, not as a surprise. The unbelief of some and the treachery of one do not catch Christ unprepared; they are part of the pattern the Father has foreordained and Christ enters willingly.
65: A revelation of God: "this is why I told you that no one can come to me unless the Father has enabled them" — Christ returns to the sovereignty theme of John 6:44 as the explanation for the departure of the unbelieving disciples. Their turning back is not a refutation of his teaching but a confirmation of it: the ones who stay are those whom the Father draws; those who leave are those for whom the enabling has not yet come — or, in the case of Judas, will never come.
66: A revelation of God: "from this time many of his disciples turned back and no longer followed him." The mass departure is the crisis-point of the Galilean ministry. The Evangelist records it without softening: the Bread of Life discourse, the most concentrated theological claim Christ has yet made, results in the greatest defection. The cross will replicate this pattern: the moment of fullest revelation is the moment of most radical rejection.
67: A revelation of God: "you do not want to leave too, do you?" — the question addressed to the Twelve is not anxious but sovereign. Christ does not renegotiate the terms of the discourse to retain his followers; he puts the same claim to the Twelve that drove away the crowd. The question reveals that discipleship is always a choice made in full knowledge of what is demanded, and that Christ will not dilute his self-disclosure to make retention easier.
68: A direct revelation: Peter's answer — "Lord, to whom shall we go? You have the words of eternal life" — is one of the great confessions of the Gospel. "The words of eternal life" picks up Christ's claim at John 6:63 (the words he speaks are spirit and life) and extends it: there is no alternative source, no rival teacher, no other word that gives what Christ's words give. The confession is not triumphant but honest — not "we want to stay" but "there is nowhere else that has what you have."
69: A direct confession: "we have come to believe and to know that you are the Holy One of God." The title ho hagios tou theou carries both Messianic freight (the anointed one set apart for God's purpose) and divine holiness freight (the one who belongs to the realm of the holy God). In the OT the "holy one" language belongs to God himself (Isaiah 40:25; Habakkuk 3:3) and to his anointed servant (Psalms 16:10). Peter's confession places Jesus in both registers at once.
70: A revelation of God: "have I not chosen you, the Twelve? Yet one of you is a devil!" — the election of the Twelve is Christ's own sovereign act (cf. John 15:16), but election does not preclude betrayal. The Evangelist does not resolve the tension; he sustains it. That the twelve were chosen by Christ and that one of the twelve is the betrayer both belong to the divine design. The one who is sovereign over election is also sovereign over the cross that betrayal makes possible.
71: A shadow: the parenthetical identification of Judas as the betrayer anticipates the typological reading the Evangelist will make explicit in John 13:18, citing Psalms 41:9 — "even my close friend, someone I trusted, one who shared my bread, has turned against me." The one who has shared the bread of the feeding miracle and has heard the Bread of Life discourse is the one who will lift his heel against Christ. The psalm of David's betrayal by a companion finds its ultimate fulfillment in the twelve's betrayal by one of its own.
